serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Murgh Yakhni is approximately 250 ml (one bowl), which is the typical portion served as a side dish or light soup course in a Kashmiri meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Murgh Yakhni exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken soup / chicken broth entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, chicken broth with added ingredients). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central chicken broth data. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available in ICMR-NIN for this preparation; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Murgh Yakhni. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate chicken broth/soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (15 × 4.5) / 100 = 0.68, rounded to 0.5 (conservative). The very low carbohydrate content (1.8 g per 100 g) and high protein content support a low GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (150 mg per 100 g); aromatic spices with no added salt in traditional preparation
hypertension.guidance: Murgh Yakhni contains approximately 150 mg sodium per 100 g (375 mg per 250 ml serving), which is within acceptable limits for most hypertensive individuals when consumed in standard portions; however, patients on strict sodium restriction (<1500 mg/day) should monitor added salt and ghee during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15), low carbohydrate content (1.8 g per 100 g), and high protein (7.5 g per 100 g) supporting postprandial glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Yakhni is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (~15), minimal carbohydrate load (4.5 g per 250 ml serving), and high protein content that slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glycaemic response; yogurt enrichment may further improve insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.2 g per 100 g), anti-inflammatory spices (fennel, ginger, cardamom), and lean protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Murgh Yakhni provides a lean protein source with low saturated fat and cardioprotective spices including fennel seeds and dry ginger, making it a heart-friendly option; individuals with cardiovascular disease should ensure preparation uses minimal added ghee or oil to keep saturated fat intake low.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory properties of ginger (sonth) and fennel seeds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dry ginger powder and fennel seeds in Murgh Yakhni have documented anti-inflammatory properties that may provide symptomatic benefit; individuals with dairy sensitivity should consider omitting yogurt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Yakhni is a traditional Kashmiri chicken broth originating from the Kashmir Valley of India. The dish is prepared by slow-simmering chicken pieces in water with whole aromatic spices including fennel seeds (saunf), dry ginger powder (sonth), green cardamom, and cinnamon, producing a light, golden broth with a delicate, warming fragrance. Unlike many Indian soups, Murgh Yakhni is not spicy or chilli-forward; instead, it is defined by its subtle, layered aromatics and clean flavour. Yogurt is often whisked in near the end of cooking to enrich the broth with a mild creaminess and gentle tang without curdling. The texture is thin to lightly viscous, and the colour ranges from pale gold to ivory depending on yogurt quantity. Nutritionally, Murgh Yakhni is a high-protein (7.5 g per 100 g), low-carbohydrate (1.8 g per 100 g), moderate-fat (4.2 g per 100 g) preparation with approximately 75 kcal per 100 g. A standard 250 ml serving provides roughly 188 kcal, 18.75 g protein, and only 4.5 g carbohydrate, making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or those seeking a nourishing, low-glycaemic meal. The broth is rich in niacin (B3), vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 from the chicken, and the spices contribute trace antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds. Murgh Yakhni is served as a side dish or starter in traditional Kashmiri wazwan feasts and is also consumed as a restorative broth during illness or convalescence. It is naturally gluten-free and halal, and is appropriate for lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Is Murgh Yakhni good for diabetes?
meal_context: Light side dish at lunch or dinner
Restorative broth during illness or convalescence

confidence_reason: No exact Murgh Yakhni entry exists in ICMR-NIN published tables. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken soup/broth data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken broth entries. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chicken broth data. Saturated fat is estimated. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water content are not available from approved sources for this specific preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for Murgh Yakhni (Kashmiri chicken broth with yogurt and whole spices) are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. Saturated fat (1.2 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central chicken broth with added fat data. All core macros (protein_g 7.5, fat_g 4.2, carbohydrate_g 1.8) are non-zero and consistent with a lean chicken broth preparation; no zero-macro justification required. Portions deduplicated: input contained three identical portion entries; output retains one canonical serving of 250 ml / 250 g. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate chicken broth entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Murgh Yakhni exists in published literature as of 2024. Kashmiri language alias (مُرج یخنؠ) preserved from input; language code 'ka' in input likely refers to Kashmiri (ISO 639-3: kas) rather than Georgian.
